quick_summary: Git is the industry-standard distributed version control system that tracks changes to code, enabling collaboration, history browsing, and safe experimentation with branches. It's a non-negotiable skill for every developer role. Interview questions test branching strategies, merge vs rebase, and conflict resolution.

detailed_content.explanation:
Git tracks changes to your files like a time machine for your code. Every time you 'commit,' Git saves a snapshot of your entire project — you can jump back to any snapshot at any time. This beats saving copies like 'project_v2_final_FINAL.zip.'

The workflow is simple: `git init` creates a new repository, `git clone` copies an existing one. Changes go through a staging area (`git add`) before being committed (`git commit`). Think of staging as a shipping dock — you organize items before loading them onto the ship (commit). This two-step process lets you craft clean, logical commits instead of dumping all changes at once.
detailed_content.example:
```bash
# Start a new project
git init my-project && cd my-project

# Make changes and stage them
echo '# My Project' > README.md
echo 'print("hello")' > main.py
git add README.md main.py

# Commit with a clear message
git commit -m 'feat: initial project setup with README and main script'

# Check history
git log --oneline
# Output: a1b2c3d feat: initial project setup with README and main script

# Make more changes
echo 'def greet(name): return f"Hello, {name}"' >> main.py
git add main.py
git commit -m 'feat: add greet function to main script'

# View diff between commits
git diff HEAD~1 HEAD

# Clone an existing repo
git clone https://github.com/octocat/Hello-World.git
```
detailed_content.key_takeaway: Write commit messages as 'what this change does' in imperative mood (feat: add, fix: resolve, refactor: move) — this matches the convention used by semantic versioning tools and makes `git log` readable.
detailed_content.try_it: Create a new git repo, make 3 commits with different types (feat, fix, docs), then use `git log --oneline` and `git diff HEAD~2 HEAD` to see the full history of changes.
detailed_content.complexity: Time: O(1) for commits, O(n) for clone where n = repository size

pro_tip: In interviews, explain that you use `git add -p` (patch mode) to stage partial changes within a file — this lets you create atomic, focused commits and shows discipline in version control hygiene.
